metadata.title: Claude, Scoped.
metadata.description: Skill teardown of the remotion-explainer — scope-before-spectacle, blueprint-first, silent-default Remotion tutorial builder.

beats.narration_text: Annyeong — this is Liam, in for Bear. The remotion-explainer skill turns one textbook concept into a short Remotion video. Scope before spectacle. Blueprint before scenes. Silent before voiced. This episode is that skill explaining its own discipline.

output: Law: one concept per video; blueprint before scenes
Pipeline: scan → storyboard → scenes → captions | voice → render

beats.narration_text: The skill folder is compact on purpose. The SKILL.md holds the whole discipline — scope rules, the seven-beat arc template, the silent-first default. A blueprint template and a scene template live in references/. The rendering machinery is not here. Rendering is two commands the human runs after the skill stops.

props.title: Blueprint before code.
props.calloutText: The skill stops at scenes + two commands — rendering is the human's step

beats.narration_text: Five stages. Scan — propose which concepts earn a video, like a cajal figure scan for motion. Storyboard — produce the blueprint.md, the seven-beat arc. Scenes — generate one Remotion scene file per beat. Then either captions by default, or voice as an opt-in upgrade. The skill hands off render commands. The human runs them.

props.title: Two commands remain for the human.

phases.label: CAPTIONS
phases.desc: on-screen text is the caption — no key

phases.label: VOICE
phases.desc: ElevenLabs opt-in — only if keys present

props.footerNote: Skill stops at scenes + commands. Rendering is the human's step.

beats.narration_text: To demo the storyboard phase — free, no render, no key — ask it: what is a photon? The skill scans the concept, proposes shapes, and outputs a blueprint. That is the gate the human approves before any scene code generates.

props.segment: Run it on a physics concept

beats.narration_text: The blueprint shows the shape. Scene one: the hook — light behaves like a wave and like a particle. Scene two: the wave evidence — Young's double slit. Scene three: the particle evidence — the photoelectric effect. Scene four: why both are true. Each scene is named, its on-screen text specified, its speaker note written. No Remotion code yet. The gate is here.

props.title: blueprint.md — remotion-explainer output
props.code:
## Blueprint — what-is-a-photon
Shape: misconception-repair · 7 scenes · ~90s

### Scene 01 — Hook
**On-screen:** "Light does two things that can't both be true."
**Speaker notes:** A photon passes through two slits at once
and lands on a detector like a bullet. Both. Every time.

### Scene 02 — Wave evidence
**On-screen:** "Double slit: one photon, two paths."
**Speaker notes:** The interference pattern appears — one
photon at a time. It interferes with itself.

### Scene 03 — Particle evidence
**On-screen:** "Photoelectric: frequency, not brightness."
**Speaker notes:** Below the threshold frequency: no electrons,
no matter how bright. Above it: electrons, instantly.

beats.narration_text: The Cajal principle lands here: scope before spectacle. The skill proposes which concepts in a chapter deserve a video — not all of them, just the ones where motion explains something static text cannot. One concept per video is the rule. Several candidates in the source material means a proposal list, then the human picks one. The skill never storyboards everything.

props.heading: Motion earns its keep.
props.body: The scan phase proposes one to three concepts that reward a sixty-to-one-twenty-second video — naming each concept, the single idea it teaches, the misconception it repairs, and a suggested shape. It recommends one to build first. This mirrors the cajal figure skill's discipline: figures worth drawing, not figures of everything. Motion that clarifies. Motion that earns its duration.
props.quote: scope before spectacle, one idea per artifact, every visual answers the narration.
props.sparkLine: If it doesn't move the idea, cut it.

beats.narration_text: The blueprint gates the code. Twenty to forty-five spoken words per scene. The seven-beat arc: hook, name, first mechanism, make the relationship visible, misconception repair, transfer, durable model. Five to eight scenes, one idea-move each. The skill stops after the blueprint and waits for approval. No scene code generates until the storyboard is accepted. This is not politeness — it is the constraint that prevents a misaligned reel.

props.heading: The storyboard is the gate.
props.body: The blueprint follows a seven-beat arc: hook, name the concept, first mechanism, make the relationship visible, misconception repair, transfer to a new domain, durable model. Five to eight scenes, twenty to forty-five spoken words each. One idea-move per scene — no decorative complexity. The skill stops here and gets the blueprint accepted before writing any Remotion code. This is the gate that prevents misaligned scenes.
props.quote: Stop here and get the blueprint accepted before writing scenes or audio.
props.sparkLine: Words before code. Code before render.

beats.narration_text: The design tell is the silent default. The skill builds captioned scenes — no API key, no quota, no spending. The on-screen text carries the meaning. Voice is an opt-in upgrade, added only when the user asks and the keys are present. The skill also never renders the MP4 itself. It stops at scenes plus two commands and says so plainly. Never imply a finished video appeared.

props.heading: Free by default. Voiced by choice.
props.body: The default output needs no API key: each scene carries its key phrase as on-screen text. Narration is added only when the user requests it and ElevenLabs credentials are set. The skill also never renders the MP4 — headless Chromium and minutes of compute belong to the human's machine. The skill hands over two commands and says this plainly. The design tell is this honesty: never claim the video exists until it does.
props.quote: A skill is a prompt; rendering needs headless Chromium and minutes of compute, and narration needs an API key and quota.

beats.narration_text: What the remotion-explainer skill actually does: it applies scope-before-spectacle to motion — proposing only the concepts that earn a video, gating on a blueprint before any code, building silent-captioned scenes by default, and stopping before rendering. The human approves the storyboard, runs two commands, and gets a Remotion reel. The skill never pretends it rendered the MP4.

artifactLines: Scope law: proposes only concepts where motion explains what text cannot.
Blueprint gate: storyboard approved before any Remotion code generates.
Silent-first: on-screen text carries meaning — no API key needed by default.
Honest handoff: skill stops at scenes + commands; never claims the MP4 exists.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Use the remotion-explainer skill on any concept you can state as a misconception. Before you open the terminal, ask: what does motion make visible that a static diagram cannot? Name the misconception before you write a single scene. Then run: ./art remotion-explainer, and your concept in quotes.

output: Use the remotion-explainer skill on any concept as a misconception.
Before you open the terminal, ask: what does motion make visible
Name the misconception before you write a single scene.
